questions.specification: Rapid prototyping is most useful when designers need to:

options.content: Minimise the risk of failure by conducting real-world product testing before full-scale production.
options.markscheme: Correct: rapid prototyping is used to quickly create and test models/prototypes to evaluate and reduce risk before committing to full-scale manufacture.

options.content: Increase manufacturing throughput by optimising assembly line cycle times.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this relates to production planning and process optimisation rather than producing and evaluating prototypes.

options.content: Reduce raw material costs by using bulk purchasing agreements.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: bulk purchasing is a procurement strategy, not the primary purpose of rapid prototyping.

options.content: Eliminate the need for user feedback by finalising design decisions early.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: rapid prototyping typically increases and accelerates user/testing feedback rather than removing it.

questions.specification:
What purpose of prototyping is demonstrated in this scenario?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXd3HaL1Oy7Md4JZvVZxr3X3vmJYh3mzxcOoIQYOvzEki7VozberiEF-XQ9LH84OXYIMYwLAQ533cLltqud4QelgKoZOOK1DMhlegbZCYQ4F_JWBMeIWbgvdswIerhbqRy86y7Bf?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: To test and evaluate the design with users/stakeholders so issues can be identified and improvements made before final manufacture
options.markscheme: Correct: this describes prototyping as a means of testing/evaluating a design prior to final production.

options.content: To prepare the final product for mass production by creating production tooling and quality-control documentation
options.markscheme: Incorrect: production tooling and QC documentation relate to manufacture planning, not the primary purpose of prototyping shown.

options.content: To reduce the unit cost of a product by selecting cheaper materials for the final design
options.markscheme: Incorrect: material cost reduction may be a later optimisation aim, but it is not the purpose of prototyping demonstrated.

options.content: To create marketing materials and branding assets before the design has been tested
options.markscheme: Incorrect: marketing assets are not the central purpose of prototyping in this context.

questions.specification: A company is designing a flat-pack wooden chair intended for mass production and easy home assembly. To support this development, the design team uses a range of modelling techniques to communicate ideas and evaluate the product’s performance.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a technical representation of the chair components, indicating the flat panels, fasteners, and joining mechanisms.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f392e501-19b2-4d85-9e79-23c43caa2e0b)

Identify the type of drawing shown in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme: - Exploded isometric drawing <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** presents the results of a finite element analysis (FEA) simulation of the chair’s seat under a 100 kg load.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/92e51901-329f-40b7-a70e-523cfa56e53a)

Outline two reasons why a designer would use FEA in the development of this product.
parts.markscheme:
- To identify areas of high stress or risk of material failure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- To optimise the design for weight and material efficiency without physical testing <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a photo of a physical prototype of the chair being tested by a user


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f017b4a7-019e-4cfa-be9e-b1d6690c1ebf)

Describe two benefits of using a physical prototype to gather user feedback during development.
parts.markscheme:
- Allows users to assess comfort and usability firsthand <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables identification of practical issues like wobbling or difficulty in assembly <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how computer-aided design (CAD) models can reduce both design time and cost in the production of the chair.
parts.markscheme:
- CAD models allow for quick iteration and modification without rebuilding prototypes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Assembly and fit can be tested virtually to prevent errors before manufacturing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables accurate part nesting for efficient use of materials in production <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss two advantages and two limitations of using 3D printed rapid prototypes for evaluating the chair’s joint components.
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage: Fast turnaround for testing fit and alignment <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Advantage: Low cost compared to machining or carpentry prototypes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitation: Material strength differs from wood, limiting load testing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitation: Size and resolution constraints of 3D printers may affect accuracy <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate how the use of exploded drawings, CAD, and physical prototypes helps the design team communicate effectively with stakeholders throughout the development of the chair.
parts.markscheme:
- Exploded drawings show component relationships clearly for manufacturers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CAD models can be rendered realistically to gain client or investor approval <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CAD simulations help technical stakeholders understand performance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Physical prototypes provide tangible feedback for users or retailers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Combining all three tools covers visual, functional, and emotional communication needs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports iterative design and early issue detection, reducing costly revisions <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What is the primary purpose of using drawings in the design process?

options.content: To communicate concepts and gather feedback

questions.specification: What is a major advantage of rapid prototyping?

options.content: Allows quick production of testable models
Eliminates the need for material testing

parts.content: State two advantages of using exploded drawings in communicating design ideas.
parts.markscheme:
- They clearly show the relationship between parts. <marks>1</marks>
- They help explain assembly or disassembly processes. <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] per correct advantage up to [2 max].</note>

questions.specification: Which statement best describes a physical prototype?

options.content: A sketch of an initial design
A tangible 3D representation of a design or system
A written report detailing design criteria

questions.specification:
What does this image demonstrate about CAD-based design processes?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/56dc852d-e029-41e8-82c4-9d5747cb7ecf.jpeg)

options.content: Allows for precise adjustment of complex geometry
Minimizes the use of physical mock-ups

questions.specification: Rapid prototyping helps designers by:
A company is designing a new office chair for long-term computer use. The design process includes visual development, user testing, and performance evaluation.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows an isometric sketch of an office chair.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a3c65884-fab7-4b9e-be9b-9f2201dc79c1)

Identify one detail from the sketch that communicates the intended function of the chair.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies a relevant functional feature shown in the sketch, e.g. castors/wheels indicating mobility around an office <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a relevant functional feature shown in the sketch, e.g. adjustable-height mechanism/lever indicating the seat height can be adjusted for different users/desks <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a relevant functional feature shown in the sketch, e.g. backrest/lumbar support indicating support for seated working posture <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Any other valid detail from the sketch linked to intended office-chair function <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain one advantage of using isometric sketches in the idea development stage.
parts.markscheme:
- They allow the designer to visually communicate 3D ideas quickly without the need for CAD software. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for any valid advantage of using isometric sketching.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a person testing an office chair in front of a computer.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e2d945d1-fecf-435c-a0c7-6d6a9516d675)

Describe two observations the designer might make while watching the user test the chair.
parts.markscheme:
- The user frequently shifts posture, indicating discomfort. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- The user adjusts the height or recline often, showing the importance of adjustability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per relevant user behaviour observed, up to [2 max].*

parts.content: Outline how observing users during testing can lead to improvements in the final design.
parts.markscheme:
- Helps identify ergonomic issues like poor lumbar support. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Allows designers to respond to real-world interactions with the product. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Encourages iterative design based on practical feedback. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid point, up to [3 max].*

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a mechanical load testing setup being used on a chair base.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8a93036f-592a-47f1-8b14-dee43b81acd5)

Explain the benefit of performing mechanical load tests on a chair prototype.
parts.markscheme:
- Confirms the chair meets weight-bearing safety standards. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Prevents structural failure during real-world use. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Supports regulatory compliance and long-term durability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Builds consumer confidence through tested performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per explained benefit, up to [4 max].*

parts.content: Discuss how combining user testing (**Figure 2**) and mechanical testing (**Figure 3**) improves the overall quality of the design.
parts.markscheme:
- User testing highlights comfort and usability; mechanical testing ensures safety and durability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Each test addresses a different aspect of performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Leads to a design that satisfies both emotional (comfort) and physical (strength) needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Helps identify compromises needed between form, function, and performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Enables data-driven decisions to finalise the most viable product. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Reduces market failure by ensuring both user satisfaction and technical compliance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid, well-developed point, up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: The main benefit of prototyping is to:

options.content: gather feedback from users and clients

parts.content: Outline the purpose of finite element analysis (FEA) in product design.
parts.markscheme:
- State that FEA simulates how a product performs under stress or load <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify that it predicts weak points or failure areas in materials <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that designers use the data to strengthen or optimise components <marks>A1</marks>
- Outline that it reduces the need for multiple physical tests <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [1] per valid point up to [4 max].</note>

questions.specification:
What is one key function of physical prototypes in design?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfscjvbN7v-Rv-shRSuV9mP_cU3ZoNxgOVRaFUGdlkpJKBlplcIcvzg-nZd0mn7L5ncj2qsf890-v2dXI1orDmC1QmgnUpOv3FkM62gehdm-q5oa-ayNgZ9H6oXu_ZPchkUs4HKwQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Display realistic photo renderings of the final product
Represent tangible versions of the design
Test form, fit, and function using a real model

questions.specification: CAD primarily allows designers to:

options.content: Create, develop, and analyse design outcomes using software

questions.specification: Why might a designer use a drawing in early stages of a project?

How does this software support designers during development?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/f9670bdb-3a4c-4373-8121-c033cd8b847a.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Digital prototyping software is focused on user interface and user experience design, rather than modelling formal organisational or team communication structures.

options.content: By visualizing, testing, and refining digital designs
options.markscheme: Correct. Prototyping software allows designers to create a visual representation of the product, test its functionality and user flow through interactive elements, and refine the design based on feedback during the development phase.

Incorrect. While digital tools can be used for wireframing, 'low-fidelity sketches' typically refers to hand-drawn or very basic conceptual drawings, whereas the software shown is used for medium to high-fidelity prototyping.
Incorrect. While colour selection is a feature within the software, it is not the primary way the software supports the overall development process compared to visualization and testing.

questions.specification: Why is FEA valuable in the prototyping phase?

options.content: It builds a final working product

parts.content: Discuss how physical prototypes differ from virtual prototypes in terms of feedback.
parts.markscheme:
- Physical prototypes provide tactile, ergonomic, and material-based feedback. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Users can interact with them in real environments. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They reveal issues that might not appear on screen (e.g., weight). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Virtual prototypes offer rapid iteration and easy alterations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They are useful for visualising design without material cost. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Some user feedback (e.g., usability) may be limited in virtual models.<marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each well-explained comparison up to [6 max].</note>

questions.specification: Physical prototypes are often created to:

options.content: Show functionality and get user feedback
Replace the need for design drawings
Reduce manufacturing risks by identifying assembly issues early

questions.specification: CAD is particularly beneficial because:

options.content: It provides tools for precise development and iteration

questions.specification: FEA is most helpful when designers want to:

parts.content: Discuss the role of prototypes in refining ideas and ensuring product success.
parts.markscheme:
- Test the form, fit, and function of the design <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify design flaws early in the development process <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Gather real-world feedback from potential users <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Facilitate communication between designers, clients, and stakeholders <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Support an iterative design process through constant refinement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduce production risks and overall development costs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increase user satisfaction and product usability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Evaluate market acceptance through user trials <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Align the final design with both technical and user requirements <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] mark for each valid discussion point, up to a maximum of [9].</note>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B. Design in practice > B2.2 Modelling and prototyping > B2.2.3 CAD modelling and design development
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B2.2.3 CAD modelling and design development
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the utility of 3D CAD models within the design process, which directly pertains to CAD modelling as a tool for design development and iteration.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A2.2 Prototyping techniques > A2.2.5 CAD for virtual prototyping
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.2.5 CAD for virtual prototyping
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: 3D CAD models are the fundamental component of virtual prototyping, allowing designers to evaluate form and function before physical production.

parts.content: Explain two reasons why 3D CAD models are useful in the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a valid reason and <marks>1 mark</marks> for an appropriate explanation or development of that reason, up to <marks>2 marks</marks> per reason. Max <marks>4 marks</marks>.

Possible reasons include:

- Realistic visualisation of products before production <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - allowing designers and stakeholders to evaluate aesthetics and functionality without the cost or time required for a physical prototype <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Easily modified to test iterations and design improvements <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - which speeds up the development cycle and reduces the cost of redesigning compared to physical modelling <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Precision and dimension control for manufacturing <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - ensuring that components fit together accurately and reducing errors in the final production stage <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Digital sharing with stakeholders for remote feedback <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - facilitating collaboration across different geographical locations and accelerating the decision-making process <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Integration with Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - models can be exported directly to CNC machines or 3D printers, which minimises human error in translating the design to production <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how feedback gathered from stakeholders influences prototype development.
parts.markscheme:
- Stakeholder feedback identifies usability or aesthetic issues. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Designers can adjust dimensions, materials, or form accordingly. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It supports iterative testing and refinement. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps ensure alignment with client expectations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Feedback can also uncover new user needs or missed features. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overall, it contributes to a more successful final design. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per explained point up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: A design team is developing a modular desk lamp for students living in small apartments. To help select the most suitable design, they use a variety of techniques and test methods at different stages of the process.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a few hand-drawn isometric concept sketches of modular desk lamps


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/95eb9d08-e95e-47e0-a91e-5032dc7f02ab)

State one design selection tool that can help compare the above lamp concepts.
parts.markscheme:
- Decision matrix <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] for a valid design selection tool.

parts.content: Outline one advantage and one limitation of using isometric sketches during design.
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage: Clearly communicates $3D$ form and proportions to stakeholders. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitation: Does not provide information about function or performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] per relevant point, up to [2 max.]

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a student using a cardboard prototype of the lamp on a small study desk in a bedroom setting.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/329c2b79-c1b7-4e6d-a9ca-f88df5ea5df8)

Identify two types of qualitative feedback the user might give during testing.
parts.markscheme:
- Ease of adjusting the lamp’s angle. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aesthetic appeal and fit with desk space. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] per valid qualitative feedback type, up to [2 max.].

parts.content: Explain how cardboard prototyping supports effective selection of design ideas.
parts.markscheme:
- Allows quick and inexpensive evaluation of scale and form. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps identify ergonomic or usability issues before final design. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages user interaction and insight into functional aspects. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Facilitates early elimination of unworkable ideas. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] per well-explained point, up to [4 max.]

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a CAD stress test simulation of the lamp’s adjustable arm under load, with red areas indicating stress concentrations.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/75fde644-f46e-4167-9803-7f625c59b321)

State the purpose of this virtual test in selecting the best design.
parts.markscheme:
- To ensure structural stability and identify potential failure points. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award [1] for a valid purpose of the simulation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate how combining physical prototyping and virtual testing supports better design selection.
parts.markscheme:
- Physical prototyping provides real-world usability and ergonomic insights. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Virtual testing offers data on material performance under stress. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Together, they offer a more holistic understanding of design feasibility. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increases confidence in selected design by validating both function and form. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces development time and cost by minimizing late-stage failures. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports stakeholder decision-making with both user feedback and simulation data. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] per well-justified point, up to [6 max.]

parts.content: Evaluate the benefits and limitations of using CAD software in modelling and prototyping.
parts.markscheme:
Benefits:

- **Identify** that CAD allows for fast changes and design iterations <marks>A1</marks>

- **State** that CAD supports accurate dimensions and tolerances <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explain** how 3D visualization enhances communication during the design process <marks>A1</marks>

- **Outline** how CAD files can be used directly for CAM or 3D printing <marks>A1</marks>

Limitations:

- **Identify** the high software and training costs associated with CAD <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explain** that CAD may not fully represent tactile feedback or material behaviour <marks>A1</marks>

- **Outline** how over-reliance on software can reduce hands-on design exploration <marks>A1</marks>

- **State** that powerful hardware is often required for large files <marks>A1</marks>

- **State** that physical validation through prototyping is still necessary <marks>A1</marks>

9 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each valid benefit or limitation identified, up to a maximum of [9].</note>

questions.specification: Physical prototypes differ from digital models because they:

options.content: Can only be used for conceptualisation
Allow for tactile interaction and real-world testing

questions.specification: Which tool helps designers show internal and external design aspects to stakeholders?
Which of the following is a digital method of design development?

Which benefit does this image illustrate?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfqNhhTf1pOASr3uBLRMvDHl85K24UwCuJKurBh7uPQ6RlpIgMjqtUKbP8KpLZiUANSO4j_O_NRQQDw8DcYbDz2JwNrg7vC5lqwjqg-aTasxcO-eQ5pX7VHPDMPTlUEuBO7uNfi0A?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Identification of structural performance using FEA

questions.specification: What does FEA allow designers to predict?
Why do designers use prototypes in user trials?

options.content: To gather user feedback and data

questions.specification:
How do prototypes aid in modern product design?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdUMvwo7klG-92e5bEWmzngnLCaEmLgl_wJVNgjwxiN0utHqPOHzOHwpbdl5YiuIPM_KY36MTtk4uZYF9HYCNj8y2QBYuVcMJMr9psoKX2lAi1DA-VYJoub1AdX_5rf5pXiM1Qo?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: They ensure color accuracy for product packaging
They enable complex parts to be printed for testing
options.markscheme: Rapid prototyping (3D printing) allows for the creation of complex geometries and internal structures that would be difficult to manufacture otherwise, facilitating functional and physical testing during the design process.

options.content: They produce aesthetic animations for clients

questions.specification:
Which purpose of sketching is illustrated here?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/152e2977-664d-4259-a5ae-94d6d8b7fcb7.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Virtual simulation of airflow is a function of Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE) or physical wind tunnel testing, not freehand sketching.
Collecting sensor data involves hardware and digital logging, whereas sketching is a 2D visualization tool.
Sketching is a low-cost, rapid method used in the early stages of the design process to explore concepts and facilitate communication and discussion between designers and stakeholders.
Developing a production line involves detailed technical planning, 3D modelling, and logistics, which occur much later in the design cycle than initial sketching.

questions.specification:
What key advantage of CAD is demonstrated by this exploded view?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXco8YFL_rvTzno6wRS1VuhauYnJAMsoOaslVmjMRclR_7yLVjX_qxypRO0mUS8pMrHEa24TvOkTx0eJUnI6Poqucg3iojSkeJ9pp15PR5tsthq932Zx2PbswctP6orgkptRv7mCCg?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Clear visualization of internal component relationships
options.markscheme: Exploded views in CAD separate parts to show how components fit and relate within an assembly.
This relates to user research/marketing communication rather than a CAD exploded view advantage.

options.content: Digital simulation of the assembly process
options.markscheme: CAD can support assembly planning, but the key advantage demonstrated specifically by an exploded view is visualizing internal parts and their relationships rather than simulating the physical assembly process.
Brand positioning is a marketing activity and is not the key advantage demonstrated by an exploded CAD view.

questions.specification: Which method provides the most accurate **digital development of a product’s form and dimensions**?

What is the primary benefit of using concept drawings in the design process?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXcbWkK-cPdp4bqQVF2HLS3i52uUAd-Kfc4w7SW_L5UKmdJVbZB7VDuS0_zJj4jyHnrG0-kx_qdL0s1gmdpZ6Mvz7PchvB1ev724-rZg_5KYDT8fI3z-qXIRR0pjahBwuhbTfdXbEA?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: They simulate structural performance under stress
They facilitate feedback and discussion of ideas

questions.specification: A company is designing a new insulated travel mug for hot drinks. The design process includes concept development, user testing, and material analysis.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a hand-drawn sketch of a travel mug design.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4af57d4d-a1f7-40e4-92f5-51dea77dd7f8)

Identify one feature of the mug visible in the sketch that suggests portability.
parts.markscheme: - A lid with a spout or flip-cap indicates it's meant for use on-the-go. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why sketching is useful in the early stages of the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- It allows quick communication of ideas before committing to digital modelling. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages idea generation and iteration. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a person holding the mug while walking outside.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7ad504af-38c3-4cec-9293-c6552967cb76)

Describe two aspects of user behaviour that a designer could observe from this test.
parts.markscheme:
- Whether the user finds the mug comfortable to grip with one hand. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- How easily the user drinks from the lid while in motion. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how user feedback during real-world testing could inform further design development.
parts.markscheme:
- Reveals usability issues, like leaks or uncomfortable grips. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can guide changes in lid mechanism or surface texture. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Highlights unexpected use cases, like fitting in car cup holders. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a thermal test being performed on the mug.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/fd5609a2-c548-4e0e-bdfc-c70f1e219571)

State one reason why heat retention testing is important for this product.
parts.markscheme: - It ensures the mug keeps beverages warm for a suitable length of time. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how both ergonomic testing (Figure 2) and thermal testing (Figure 3) contribute to a successful final product.
parts.markscheme:
- Ergonomic testing ensures comfort and usability in daily life. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Thermal testing ensures the mug performs its core function - insulation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Combined, they validate both user satisfaction and technical performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps balance physical feel and material capability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces customer complaints and increases market acceptance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Informs final adjustments before manufacturing. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the difference between isometric and orthographic projection.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that isometric projection is a 3D pictorial representation where all dimensions are drawn at full scale <marks>A1</marks>

- **State** that orthographic projection represents a 3D object using multiple 2D views, such as the front, top, and side views <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each clearly explained difference up to [2 max].</note>

questions.specification:
What does FEA primarily allow designers to do?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdt3lMoYGqV5FhMlLqtl1n0yYWpzmOItksw7H402Yw9zPpVXaO8r95tU50PXqwp1xHF_tJ6fgy8DPD5le7EOjgdLxjQZG7XZzYMFpG4pXW1trm-7XC-CkI_xDaLKC4beOMEs-u76Q?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Generate photorealistic renderings of a product concept
Simulate performance under stress and pressure
Produce 2D technical drawings from a 3D model

questions.specification: Which of the following is NOT a feature of CAD software?

parts.content: Describe how prototypes can be used to evaluate a product’s functionality.
parts.markscheme:
- Prototypes simulate the final product to test real-world performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Feedback from testing helps identify usability or ergonomic issues. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adjustments can be made before final production based on prototype feedback. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They allow exploration of different materials and constructions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each valid description of a functional use of prototypes up to [4 max].*

questions.specification: Which technique simulates how parts respond to stress or pressure?
A company is developing a new foldable standing desk for remote workers. The design process involves sketching, testing material strength, and evaluating functionality.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows an isometric sketch of a foldable standing desk.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a79d31ef-b536-4463-bdb2-2123eddd0f9c)
i.) Identify one functional feature of the desk visible in the sketch.
parts.markscheme:
Foldable frame with visible hinges for easy storage. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for a feature clearly related to the intended functionality.*

parts.content: ii.) Outline two reasons why isometric sketching is beneficial when selecting a design idea.
parts.markscheme:
Allows 3D visualisation of the product from a realistic angle. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Helps communicate key dimensions and layout to stakeholders. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per relevant reason, up to [2 max].*

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a user adjusting the desk height using a manual crank handle.



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c701e608-8cf0-4514-a04c-e1a8e89bf06a)

i.) Describe how this physical interaction could be tested in a user trial.
parts.markscheme:
Observe the ease of motion and effort required to turn the crank. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Record feedback on comfort, noise, and responsiveness. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Measure time taken to switch between sitting and standing modes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid point, up to [3 max].*

parts.content: ii.) Explain how insights from this user testing can influence design selection.
parts.markscheme:
Helps eliminate concepts that are hard to operate. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Prioritises options with more user-friendly adjustment mechanisms. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Could lead to replacing crank with electric motor if necessary. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Informs final concept choice based on overall ease-of-use. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per distinct, well-explained impact, up to [4 max].*

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a technician applying force to test the load-bearing capacity of the desk surface.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/72741f84-3e9c-4a38-9424-fd156e21f1e3)

State one reason for testing the load-bearing strength of the desk.
parts.markscheme:
To ensure it can safely hold equipment like monitors and laptops. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for any relevant purpose of strength testing.*

parts.content: Evaluate the role of both user trials and mechanical testing in selecting a final design.
parts.markscheme:
User trials assess usability, comfort, and satisfaction. <marks>1 mark</marks>

 Mechanical testing ensures safety, reliability, and durability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Together, they balance form and function. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Prevent poor decisions based solely on aesthetics. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Enable objective comparison of design concepts. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Improve stakeholder confidence in chosen solution. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clearly explained point, up to [6 max].*
